messages.content:
Hey — I can see you've already got a full policy set in place. Solid setup too, with the domain separation, sensitivity tiers, conservative autonomy on compliance and customers, the whole nine yards.

What are we updating today?
i was hoping you could give me some insight about including 3rd party testing as part of our CI gate? Currently all tests are made by the same agent who builds the code, which is a trust-loop.

Yeah, you're putting your finger on a real problem. When the same agent writes the code and writes the tests, you get a circular validation loop — the agent's blind spots show up in both layers. If it misunderstands a compliance requirement, the test it writes will validate the misunderstanding. You pass all gates and ship a bug that looks like a feature.

For a project like yours — PCI-DSS, SOX, AML/KYC, data privacy — that's not a theoretical risk, it's a structural one. A regulator isn't going to be impressed that your tests passed if the tests were generated by the same system that wrote the code.

There are a few angles here, and they're not mutually exclusive:

**Third-party infrastructure/compliance scanners in CI** — tools like Checkov, tfsec, Trivy, or Snyk IaC that validate against an independent compliance baseline. They don't rely on your test logic at all — they have their own rule sets for things like "is PII encrypted at rest," "are secrets hardcoded," "does this config meet PCI requirements." They catch what self-generated tests structurally cannot. Given your stack is TypeScript on Node (not Terraform/CloudFormation), the most relevant ones for you would be something like Snyk for dependency and code scanning, or Trivy for broader vulnerability scanning. Checkov and tfsec are more IaC-focused, so they'd be less directly useful unless you add infrastructure-as-code later.

**Separating the test-writing agent from the code-writing agent** — this is the other half. Your QA role already exists as a read-everything, write-only-to-qa/reports auditor. But right now it validates *after* the fact. You could extend the model so that the QA agent (or a dedicated test-writing agent) is the one producing tests for code written by domain agents. That breaks the circular trust at the agent level rather than just the tooling level.

Both approaches strengthen different parts of the chain. The scanner gives you an independent baseline that no agent authored. The agent separation gives you adversarial test coverage where the test writer isn't anchored to the same assumptions as the code writer.

For your setup, I'd recommend both — a scanner like Snyk or Trivy added as a CI step and quality gate, plus a policy convention that domain agents don't write their own integration tests (unit tests are fine, but integration tests that validate cross-boundary behavior and compliance requirements come from QA or a dedicated test agent).

What's your instinct on this — do you want to go with just the scanner, just the agent separation, or both? And do you have a preference on which scanner, or want me to recommend based on what fits your stack best?

Let's go with both. Recommend the scanner you think fits best for our stack and add it as a CI gate. And add a convention that domain agents don't write their own integration tests — those come from QA.

Smart call — belt and suspenders. For your stack I'd go with **Snyk**. Here's why: you're a TypeScript/Node monorepo with no infrastructure-as-code right now, so the IaC-focused tools (Checkov, tfsec) would be scanning air. Snyk gives you dependency vulnerability scanning, code analysis for security anti-patterns, and it has solid Node/TypeScript support out of the box. It slots right into your GitHub Actions CI pipeline. If you add IaC later, Snyk covers that too, so you're not swapping tools down the road.

Trivy would be the runner-up — it's excellent and free — but Snyk's code analysis layer is more relevant for catching things like hardcoded secrets, PII exposure patterns, and insecure crypto usage in application code, which maps directly to your sensitivity tiers.

So here's what I'm planning to update:

**New CI quality gate — Snyk security scan.** Added as a required gate in governance.json, scoped to all domains. Runs in CI alongside your existing typecheck/lint/test gates. No change ships without passing the Snyk scan. This gives you an independent compliance baseline that no agent authored.

**New convention — integration tests written by QA only.** Domain agents (payments, compliance, customers, onboarding, gateway, dashboard) write their own unit tests, but integration tests must be written by the QA agent. This breaks the circular trust loop at the agent level. The existing convention requiring integration tests for regulated domains stays — it just shifts *who* writes them.

**QA role update.** The QA role gets updated to include write access to integration test files across domains (the `tests/` directories), and its responsibilities expand to include writing integration tests, not just validating after the fact. Its append-only restriction stays for `qa/reports/`, but it gains write access to `*/tests/` paths for integration test authorship.

Everything else in the current policy stays as-is — all existing conventions, principles, roles, autonomy levels, escalation triggers, sensitivity tiers, all preserved verbatim.

Sound right?

  "handoff_prompt": "Call aegis_policy_summary now — do not take any other action until you have called this tool and the user has confirmed Aegis governance. Select the construction role for this initial build. This is ClearFinTech, a PCI-DSS/SOX/AML-governed payment processing platform for SMBs. The governance was just updated: a Snyk security scan was added as a required CI quality gate for independent third-party validation, a new convention requires all integration tests to be written by the QA agent (not domain agents) to break the circular trust loop, and the QA role now has write access to */tests/ directories across all domains for integration test authorship. Read the full .agentpolicy/ directory as your blueprint, then build the complete project starting with shared interfaces and the data/compliance/customers foundations — those define the security boundaries and tokenization contracts everything else depends on. Before committing, call aegis_complete_task to run quality gates and close the construction session.",

  "mcp_install": "npm install -g aegis-mcp-server",
  "future_session_prompt": "Call aegis_policy_summary now. This is your governance contract — it defines your role, your boundaries, and which tools to use. Do not take any action until you have called this tool and received confirmation from the user to proceed."
}
